evidence_quotes.quote: the manufactured difficulty of cancelling some subscriptions for all kinds of services, with video streaming ones coming to mind in particular.
not explicitely displayed by most if not all platforms using such subscription service.
video game publishers can simply make you unable to access a video game that you purchased

paragraphs.text: Greetins and thanks to the EU commission for implementing ever more consumer protection laws. I wanted to leave my feedback regarding this initiative as I feel affected by it. Particularly regarding points 2, 4 and 6. Being a video game player myself, my feedback will mostly revolve around this topic, although I also agree with part 4 regarding the manufactured difficulty of cancelling some subscriptions for all kinds of services, with video streaming ones coming to mind in particular. I would also add that I would really appreciate if the automatic renewal of subscriptions would be required to be disabled by default by law. Which, in my opinion, is the main culprit behind the difficulty of cancelling subscription. You wouldn't need to go through the (often) difficult ordeal of cancelling it if it wasn't renewed without your explicit consent, which is what most platforms are doing. People who are less tech-savvy can easily be trapped by that scheme and end up paying every month for a subscription they don't use anymore because automatic renewal is not only a default parameter, but also not explicitely displayed by most if not all platforms using such subscription service. I would also add that some services (i.e most of them) will offer "free trials" while still requiring you to input your credit card information to automatically renew your "free" subscription into a payed one without telling you. Which in my opinion, is nothing short of a scam practice. A free trial should be free, and should not automatically charge you after it ends. Especially when the service is often designed to hide that information from you. So you think you're trying something for free (which is technically true) but in reality it is mostly a bait designed to charge unsuspecting people the following months using automatic subscription renewal as a vector for it. Now in regards to point 2, I much appreciate the willingness to combat gambling-style features in video games, but I would also warn the commissions to make the distinction between different forms of gambling features. There are video games that will compel people into spending real money for gambling-like features, while some games will only require in-game currency that you could obtain at a reasonable pace simply by plaing the game, and some other games feature both styles at once. I think it's an important distinction as some games, like the popular genre of "gacha" games can propose such gambling features while still being reasonable towards the user. The example of the original Overwatch game by the studio Blizzard also comes to mind, where you could use the gambling feature without spending any real money on the game and get nice rewards out of it. My point is that while the initiative is welcomed, the commission would do well to not put everything into the same basket and call it a day, as it could easily harm very respectful and non-predatory games that use similar systems. Gambling in itself can be fun, it's only dangerous when it involves real money and is designed to use every psychological manipulation to get someone to spend. I think it's predatory practices and psychological manipulations that should be fought, not gambling itself. Although, admittedly, those are sometimes harder to define. As for point 6, I cannot agree enough that the situation where video game publishers can simply make you unable to access a video game that you purchased needs to stop. Especially for singleplayer games where internet connection and server access should not be required. But also for multiplayer games, where there are countless ways for video game publishers to deal with the issue without it being cost-prohibitive to them. The consumers should be entitled to enjoy the product they've purchased without the financial incentive of a company getting in the way. Especially when alternatives exist that would not involve much upkeep cost on the part of these companies. Thank you.
